skill.name: explore-code
skill.description: Creates conservative exploratory code-change plans for deep learning research repositories. It helps authorized users identify candidate edit targets, plan reversible adaptations, and document exploratory changes without treating them as verified results.
skill.summary: Plan isolated, reversible exploratory code changes for research repositories.
skill.author: lllllllama

security_audit.summary: AI review dismissed the static command-execution, weak-crypto, sensitive-file, reconnaissance, and obfuscation hits as false positives from markdown backticks, ordinary strings, or Python type syntax. The skill does include executable helper scripts and filesystem traversal of a user-selected repository, and it references shared files outside the skill directory. No evidence of malicious intent, network exfiltration, prompt injection, or shell command execution was found.

medium_findings.title: External Relative References Outside Skill Directory
medium_findings.description: SKILL.md directs the agent to read shared reference files through parent-directory paths. The paths are fixed and not user controlled, but marketplace packaging should verify those referenced files are present and trusted.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The parent-directory references are visible in the skill instructions. The risk is integrity and dependency confusion, not confirmed malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Repository Filesystem Traversal and Path Reads
medium_findings.description: The planning script resolves user-supplied file paths for JSON inputs and recursively walks the selected repository to score candidate files. This is consistent with the skill purpose, but users should understand it inspects local repository structure.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The code directly resolves paths, reads JSON files, and walks repository files. No network transmission or command execution was found.

low_findings.title: Static Command-Execution Hits Are Documentation and String False Positives
low_findings.description: The reported backtick and shell-execution locations are markdown inline code spans or strings used in generated planning text. The Python helper imports no subprocess module and does not execute shell commands.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found formatting backticks and string interpolation, not Ruby backticks or shell execution. The script prints plans and JSON only.

low_findings.title: Static Weak-Cryptography Hits Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged lines do not implement cryptography. They contain descriptive metadata, an argument parser description, and skill prose.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: No hashing, encryption, or cryptographic API appears at the flagged locations. The scanner matched unrelated text.

low_findings.title: Static Sensitive-File and Obfuscation Hits Are False Positives
low_findings.description: The sensitive-file hit is a dictionary key iteration, and the obfuscation hit is the Python shebang line. No certificate material, encoded payload, or obfuscated execution path was found.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The reviewed lines are benign Python syntax and normal data handling. There is no semantic evidence of secret handling or obfuscation.

low_findings.title: Static Reconnaissance Hits Are Policy False Positives
low_findings.description: The flagged lines are policy guidance about when not to use the skill. They do not collect host, network, process, or environment information.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: Manual review found section headings and authorization guidance only. No reconnaissance command or system inventory behavior is present.

dangerous_patterns.title: User-Supplied Filesystem Paths Are Resolved and Read
dangerous_patterns.description: Optional JSON input paths are resolved and read from the local filesystem. This supports the planner, but it should only be used with trusted paths.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The path reads are explicit and easy to verify. Risk depends on how callers provide paths and permissions.

dangerous_patterns.title: Recursive Repository File Enumeration
dangerous_patterns.description: The helper recursively enumerates repository files to choose candidate edit targets. This is expected behavior, but it exposes repository structure in output.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The code walks files and returns relative target paths. No network sink or unauthorized upload was found.

content.value_statement: Research code changes can blur baseline work and exploratory ideas. This skill guides isolated, reversible candidate changes and records clear evidence for review.
actual_capabilities: Identifies likely code and configuration targets in a research repository.
Builds a minimal patch plan for exploratory module or adapter changes.
Separates candidate work from trusted baseline and reproduction workflows.
Produces rollback-aware summaries for exploratory code changes.
Plans smoke checks for syntax, import resolution, configuration paths, and model surfaces.
Records source anchors and comparison context for later human review.
limitations: It does not prove scientific validity or reproduction success.
It does not execute training runs or verify metrics by itself.
It assumes the user has authorized exploratory changes on an isolated branch.
It depends on repository structure and supplied research context for useful target selection.

use_cases.title: Prototype a Model Adapter
use_cases.description: Plan a small adapter or LoRA change while keeping the trusted baseline untouched.

use_cases.title: Review Migration Scope
use_cases.description: Map likely files and rollback steps before transplanting a module from another experiment.

use_cases.title: Prepare Exploratory Evidence
use_cases.description: Collect clear change summaries and validation plans before deciding whether an idea deserves full testing.

prompt_templates.title: Find Candidate Targets
prompt_templates.prompt: Use explore-code to identify likely candidate edit targets for this authorized exploratory branch. Keep the baseline unchanged.

prompt_templates.title: Plan a Small Adapter Change
prompt_templates.prompt: Use explore-code to plan a reversible adapter or LoRA insertion for the current research context. Include rollback notes and smoke checks.

prompt_templates.title: Transplant a Module Carefully
prompt_templates.prompt: Use explore-code to plan a source-anchored module transplant. Map target files, configuration wiring, validation checks, and candidate-only limits.

prompt_templates.title: Audit an Exploratory Changeset
prompt_templates.prompt: Use explore-code to summarize this exploratory changeset for review. Separate evidence, rollback steps, validation gaps, and unverified claims.

output_examples.input: A researcher asks for a conservative LoRA experiment on an isolated branch.
output: Candidate targets are grouped by model, configuration, and entrypoint relevance.
The plan recommends one reversible adapter path before broader rewrites.
Rollback notes explain which wiring and configuration changes to remove.

output_examples.input: A team wants to transplant a backbone from a related experiment.
output: The response identifies likely source and destination surfaces.
It marks metric and evaluation files as protected unless explicitly authorized.
It lists smoke checks for imports, constructor compatibility, and configuration paths.

output_examples.input: A lead asks whether an exploratory branch can be treated as a verified result.
output: The response separates candidate evidence from trusted conclusions.
It highlights validation gaps and avoids claiming reproduction success.
It recommends follow-up testing before any scientific claim is accepted.

best_practices: Use the skill only after exploratory changes are explicitly authorized.
Run it on an isolated branch or worktree with a clear comparison anchor.
Review generated plans before editing protected evaluation or metric code.
anti_patterns: Do not use it for trusted baseline reproduction or conservative debugging.
Do not treat planned candidate changes as verified scientific findings.
Do not run it against unrelated repositories without a specific research goal.

faq.question: Does this skill execute training jobs?
faq.answer: No. It plans exploratory code changes and validation steps, but it does not run training by itself.

faq.question: Can it modify the trusted baseline?
faq.answer: It should not modify the trusted baseline by default. It is designed for isolated branches or worktrees.

faq.question: What kinds of changes does it support?
faq.answer: It supports candidate module transplants, backbone adaptations, adapter insertion, head replacement, and related low-risk migration planning.

faq.question: Does it prove an experiment worked?
faq.answer: No. It treats outputs as candidate evidence that still needs testing and human review.

faq.question: Why does it inspect repository files?
faq.answer: It scores code and configuration files so it can suggest likely edit targets for the requested research context.

faq.question: Which tools can use it?
faq.answer: The metadata lists Claude, Codex, and Claude Code as supported tools.
